methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

sinks: scaledReserves used in all invariant calculations
data_flows.trust_changes: User-supplied data directly controls decimal scaling without full validation
exposure_risks: Incorrect scaling propagates to all LP/reserve/rate calculations
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL: decodeWellData has a copy-paste bug — second check tests `decimal0 == 0` instead of `decimal1 == 0`. If decimal1 is passed as 0, it remains 0 (not defaulted to 18), causing reserves[1] to be scaled by 10^18 instead of the correct factor. This corrupts all downstream calculations including LP token supply, reserve computation, and rate calculations.

transformations: D invariant Newton convergence: D[j+1] = (Ann*sum + dP*N)*D / ((Ann-1)*D + (N+1)*dP)
sinks: lpTokenSupply returned to callers (calcReserveAtRatioSwap, calcRate, etc.)
data_flows.trust_changes: Scaled reserves are trusted after scaling; no bounds check on magnitude
exposure_risks: No convergence revert — if 255 iterations exhausted, returns last (potentially wrong) value
manipulation_risks: If one reserve is 0 and the other is non-zero, the initial check passes but dP calculation divides by (scaledReserves[0] * N) which is 0, causing revert. This is an unhandled edge case that can cause DoS for pools with one empty reserve.

data_flows.source: reserves[], ratios[], j index, data (caller input)
data_flows.trust_changes: LUT data is hardcoded and trusted; ratios from caller are scaled but not bounds-checked
exposure_risks: Silent return of 0 if no convergence in 255 iterations
manipulation_risks: If scaledRatios[j] is 0, targetPrice calculation divides by zero.
If targetPrice is outside LUT range, the LUT reverts, which is correct but may DoS legitimate operations at extreme prices.
Non-convergence silently returns 0, which could be exploited by callers expecting a valid reserve value.

data_flows.source: reserves[], ratios[], j index, data (caller input)
transformations: LUT-based initial reserve estimate from fixed reserve[i]
calcRate recalculates LP supply each iteration
data_flows.trust_changes: Trusts that input reserves[i] is reasonable; LUT data hardcoded
exposure_risks: LP supply recalculated each iteration may cause oscillation
manipulation_risks: Non-convergence silently returns 0, which could be interpreted as a valid output.
If reserves[i] is very small or zero, scaledReserves[j] will also be zero/tiny, potentially causing division-by-zero in subsequent calcRate calls.

data_flows.trust_changes: CRITICAL: No msg.sender check — anyone can call upgradeTo. Only validates new implementation is registered in aquifer.
exposure_risks: Any address can upgrade the well to any aquifer-registered implementation
manipulation_risks: CRITICAL: upgradeTo and upgradeToAndCall lack onlyOwner or onlyProxy modifiers. Any user can upgrade the proxy to any well implementation registered in the same aquifer, potentially changing the well's logic maliciously (e.g., to a well with backdoor functions). The OwnableUpgradeable inheritance provides __Ownable_init but onlyOwner is never applied to upgrade functions.

transformations: Numerical derivative: rate = reserves[i] - calcReserve(reserves_with_j_incremented, i, ...)
sinks: rate value used in convergence checks
validation_points: None — no validation that rate is reasonable
exposure_risks: Precision loss for very large reserves where 1e6 perturbation is negligible
manipulation_risks: For very large scaled reserves, adding PRICE_PRECISION (1e6) to an 18-decimal value may cause the rate calculation to return 0 due to precision loss in the Newton iteration, leading to incorrect price readings.

core_rules: LP token supply D is computed via Newton's method convergence
Reserve calculation uses quadratic Newton iteration
Rate is computed as numerical derivative (finite difference with PRICE_PRECISION step)
LUT provides initial estimates for Newton convergence in ratio-targeting functions
Decimal scaling normalizes all tokens to 18 decimals internally
Wells are deployed via Aquifer using EIP-1167 minimal proxy pattern
economic_incentives: LPs deposit tokens and receive LP tokens proportional to their share of D
Traders swap tokens with the pool rebalancing per the StableSwap curve
Amplification parameter A=100 (with 2 decimal precision, effective A=1) keeps prices near parity
governance_mechanisms: WellUpgradeable uses UUPS pattern for upgrades
OwnableUpgradeable provides owner role via init
Aquifer acts as factory and registry for valid well implementations

edge_cases.scenario: decodeWellData called with decimal1=0 (e.g., data = abi.encode(6, 0))
edge_cases.expected_behavior: decimal1 should default to 18, same as decimal0
edge_cases.actual_behavior: decimal1 remains 0 because the second check is `if (decimal0 == 0)` instead of `if (decimal1 == 0)`. This causes reserves[1] to be scaled by 10^18, massively inflating its value in all calculations.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: If a well is deployed with decimal1=0 in its data encoding, all LP supply calculations, reserve calculations, and rate calculations will be severely corrupted. This could allow an attacker to drain the pool by exploiting the incorrect invariant calculations. Even if not directly exploitable due to Well construction constraints, any integration passing 0 for decimal1 will break entirely.

edge_cases.scenario: One reserve is zero, the other is non-zero
edge_cases.expected_behavior: calcLpTokenSupply should handle gracefully or revert clearly
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The function passes the initial `if (reserves[0] == 0 && reserves[1] == 0)` check but then divides by `scaledReserves[0] * N` which is 0, causing an unhandled revert
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Can cause DoS in any function that calls calcLpTokenSupply when one reserve is depleted. Could prevent withdrawals or other operations in edge pool states.

edge_cases.scenario: Newton's method doesn't converge in calcReserveAtRatioSwap or calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Function should revert with a descriptive error (like calcReserve does)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Function silently returns 0 (Solidity default for uint256 return value) since there is no revert after the for loop
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A return value of 0 could be misinterpreted by callers (e.g., Beanstalk protocol) as a valid reserve value. This could lead to incorrect pricing, unfair trades, or protocol-level accounting errors. An attacker could craft inputs that prevent convergence to receive a 0 reserve output.

edge_cases.scenario: upgradeTo called by non-owner address with a valid aquifer-registered implementation
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Only the owner should be able to upgrade the implementation
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Any address can call upgradeTo because _authorizeUpgrade has no msg.sender check and upgradeTo has no onlyOwner modifier
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker can register a malicious well implementation in the aquifer (by boring a new well with a malicious implementation), then upgrade any WellUpgradeable proxy to this new implementation. The new implementation could contain functions to drain funds, change ownership, or manipulate pool state.

edge_cases.scenario: Price exactly at LUT boundary (e.g., price = 0.001083e6)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The boundary check is `price < 0.001083e6` for revert, so price = 0.001083e6 falls into the first valid bracket which returns data for range 0.27702e6 down to 0.001083e6. This is correct.

edge_cases.scenario: updateReserve underflow when step size exceeds current reserve
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Should handle gracefully or limit step size
edge_cases.actual_behavior: If pd.targetPrice > pd.currentPrice significantly, the subtraction `reserve - pd.maxStepSize * (pd.targetPrice - pd.currentPrice) / (pd.lutData.highPrice - pd.lutData.lowPrice)` could underflow and revert in Solidity 0.8.x
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Could cause DoS for calcReserveAtRatioSwap/calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity at certain price targets where the Newton step is too aggressive.

edge_cases.expected_behavior: Correct computation of c and b parameters
edge_cases.actual_behavior: c = lpTokenSupply * lpTokenSupply / (reserves * N) * lpTokenSupply * A_PRECISION / (Ann * N) — the intermediate `lpTokenSupply * lpTokenSupply` can overflow for lpTokenSupply > ~2^128. With 18 decimal tokens, this limits total pool size.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: In practice, pool values are unlikely to approach these limits, but extreme edge cases with high-decimal dust could theoretically trigger this.

edge_cases.scenario: calcReserveAtRatioSwap with j > 1 or invalid index
edge_cases.actual_behavior: No validation on j; if j >= 2, array access would revert with out-of-bounds, but the error message is unhelpful
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Low — would just revert, no fund loss

edge_cases.actual_behavior: pd.targetPrice = scaledRatios[i] * PRICE_PRECISION / scaledRatios[j] causes division by zero revert

edge_cases.scenario: Large price gap in LUT between 0.001083e6 and 0.213318e6 (swap) or 0.27702e6 (liquidity)
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Linear interpolation should provide reasonable initial estimate
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The interpolation across such a wide range may produce a very poor initial estimate, causing Newton's method to require many more iterations or fail to converge
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Could be exploited to cause non-convergence (silent return 0) for prices in the extreme low range

assumption_violations: decodeWellData assumes both decimal default checks are correct, but the second check is buggy (checks decimal0 instead of decimal1)
calcReserveAtRatioSwap/calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity assume Newton's method always converges within 255 iterations — no fallback if it doesn't
The upgrade mechanism assumes _authorizeUpgrade is sufficient for access control, but it lacks sender verification
calcLpTokenSupply assumes neither reserve is individually zero when sumReserves > 0
The A parameter from the LUT is assumed to match the hardcoded LUT data — if a different LUT is provided, the A parameter and the precomputed data will be inconsistent
_calcRate assumes PRICE_PRECISION (1e6) is a meaningful perturbation relative to reserve magnitude — breaks for reserves >> 1e18
game_theory_risks: Missing access control on upgradeTo allows any user to upgrade the well to a different (potentially malicious) implementation registered in the aquifer
Silent return of 0 from non-converging ratio calculations could be exploited by MEV searchers who can craft specific reserve/ratio combinations

The decodeWellData bug with decimal1=0 could be used to create a well where one token's reserves are inflated by 10^18 in calculations, allowing the attacker to extract value from mismatched invariant calculations
integration_risks: External protocols calling calcReserveAtRatioSwap/calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity may receive 0 on non-convergence and treat it as valid
The Aquifer factory is trusted to correctly map implementations — if compromised, all upgradeable wells are at risk
Pumps (oracles) rely on correct rate calculations; the decodeWellData bug could feed incorrect rates to oracles
Tokens with 0 decimals configured will trigger the decodeWellData bug path, causing catastrophic miscalculation

calculation_errors.description: Line `if (decimal0 == 0) { decimal1 = 18; }` should be `if (decimal1 == 0) { decimal1 = 18; }`. Copy-paste error causes decimal1 to never be defaulted to 18.
calculation_errors.impact: All calculations using decimal1=0 will scale reserves[1] by 10^18 instead of the correct factor, corrupting LP supply, reserve, and rate calculations.

calculation_errors.description: No revert on non-convergence after 255 iterations. Function returns the last computed (potentially incorrect) lpTokenSupply value.
calculation_errors.impact: Incorrect LP supply could lead to economic exploitation.

calculation_errors.description: No revert on non-convergence. Functions return Solidity default 0.
calculation_errors.impact: Downstream consumers may interpret 0 as a valid reserve, leading to incorrect economic decisions.

token_semantics.notes: Well function supports tokens with 0-18 decimals via decodeWellData scaling. CRITICAL BUG: decimal1=0 is not properly defaulted to 18 due to copy-paste error in decodeWellData. Tokens with fee-on-transfer are not accounted for in reserve calculations.
LP token inherits from Well → ERC20Upgradeable with ERC20Permit. Supply determined by StableSwap D invariant.

invariants.name: stableswap_invariant
invariants.description: 4*A*(b0+b1) + D = 4*A*D + D^3/(4*b0*b1). Must hold at all times for pool consistency.

invariants.name: lp_supply_conservation
invariants.description: Total LP token supply must equal sum of all user LP balances.

invariants.name: decimal_scaling_correctness
invariants.description: VIOLATED: decimal1=0 is not defaulted correctly, breaking the scaling invariant.

invariants.name: newton_convergence_guarantee
invariants.description: VIOLATED in calcReserveAtRatioSwap/calcReserveAtRatioLiquidity: no revert on non-convergence.

invariants.name: upgrade_access_control
invariants.description: VIOLATED: upgradeTo/upgradeToAndCall lack onlyOwner checks, allowing unauthorized upgrades.
